title: The Epilogue
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Part 1: God's Assessment of Job and His Friends (Job 42:7-9)
subpoints: God addresses Eliphaz by name — the recognized leader — and declares His wrath against all three friends
The friends have not spoken what is right about God; Job has — specifically regarding the point that suffering is not always caused by personal sin
Elihu is notably absent from God's rebuke, suggesting his counsel was correct
Job was wrong in his accusations against God but right in his refutation of retribution theology

outline.title: Part 2: Job's Intercession for His Friends (Job 42:8-9)
subpoints: The friends must bring burnt offerings to God and have Job pray for them — a remarkable reversal
Job is called to serve as intercessor for those who wronged and tormented him throughout the book
This mirrors Christ, who was sinned against yet intercedes for His people before the Father
The friends' immediate obedience to God's command demonstrates genuine repentance

outline.title: Part 3: Job's Restoration (Job 42:10-17)
subpoints: God restores Job's losses when he prays for his friends — restoration follows intercession
Job receives twice as much as he had before: 14,000 sheep, 6,000 camels, 1,000 yoke of oxen
He also receives seven sons and three daughters — the daughters are named, a sign of honor
Job lives 140 more years, seeing four generations; the restoration is abundant but does not erase grief over lost children
God's disproportionate response of blessing points to the eternal weight of glory awaiting all righteous sufferers

outline.title: Three Final Lessons from the Book of Job
subpoints: Lesson 1: The righteous do suffer in this life — suffering is not always tied to personal sin; this helps us be more compassionate toward those in pain
Lesson 2: Job teaches us how to be spiritually prepared for suffering through ordinary means of grace: Word, prayer, sacrament, fellowship
Lesson 3: Job ultimately points us to Jesus — the righteous one who suffered for our sins, equipping us to suffer well by His example and power

summary:
This final lesson in the Lessons on Job series covers the epilogue of the book (Job 42:7-17), where God delivers His verdict, restores Job, and displays the riches of His grace even toward those who counseled wrongly. The teacher notes that this is the 11th lesson in the series and that the text shifts back from poetry to narrative prose, signaling the close of the book.

The epilogue divides into three sections. In the first, God rebukes the three friends through Eliphaz, declaring that they have not spoken rightly about Him as Job has. The specific issue on which Job was right is his insistence that suffering is not always caused by personal sin — the core claim the friends were making. Elihu's absence from the rebuke confirms that his contribution to the book was theologically sound. In the second section, God instructs the friends to bring burnt offerings and have Job intercede for them. This is a profound reversal: those who tormented Job must now rely on his prayers. The teacher draws an extended comparison to Christ, who was sinned against and yet stands before the Father interceding for His people, and who was called to be a priest offering sacrifice on their behalf.

In the third section, God restores Job with double what he had before. The teacher acknowledges that some commentators are disappointed by this ending, feeling it undermines the book's message by rewarding righteousness. But he argues instead that Job's restoration illustrates a universal principle: God promises a disproportionate response of blessing for righteous suffering, and that blessing ultimately awaits believers in eternity. The session closes with three summary lessons from the entire book: that the righteous suffer and we should be compassionate; that ordinary means of grace prepare us for suffering; and that Job, more than anything else, points us to Jesus Christ — the ultimate righteous sufferer whose example and power enable His people to suffer well.
transcript:
## Introduction: Finishing the Book of Job

The goal tonight is to finish the book of Job, and it's looking like it might actually happen. Turn to Job 42, verses 7 through 17.

This is the longest book I've preached through. We did not go verse by verse, but we covered every major category in the book across eleven sermons — and I feel a sense of real accomplishment. I've started preparing for a series on Isaiah 40 through 66 several times and can never quite work up the courage, partly because of the sheer length.

If you look at the formatting of your Bible when you reach chapter 42, you'll notice a change: the text shifts back from the poetic indentation of the dialogue sections to prose narrative. We are back in narrative, with mostly God speaking and people saying yes.

The epilogue divides naturally into three parts: verses 7 through 9, God's assessment of Job and his friends; the second section, Job's intercession for his friends; and the third and longest section, verses 10 through 17, Job's restoration.

## Part 1: God's Assessment of Job and His Friends (Job 42:7–9)

> *"And so it was, after the Lord had spoken these words to Job, that the Lord said to Eliphaz the Temanite, 'My wrath is aroused against you and your two friends, for you have not spoken of Me what is right, as My servant Job has. Now therefore, take for yourselves seven bulls and seven rams, go to My servant Job, and offer up for yourselves a burnt offering; and My servant Job shall pray for you. For I will accept him, lest I deal with you according to your folly; because you have not spoken of Me what is right, as My servant Job has.' So Eliphaz the Temanite and Bildad the Shuhite and Zophar the Naamathite went and did as the Lord commanded them; for the Lord had accepted Job."* (Job 42:7–9)

After accepting Job's repentance in verse 6 — where Job said "I abhor myself and repent in dust and ashes" — God turns from Job to the friends.

**Two preliminary observations:** First, God's assessment applies to all three of Job's counselors, but He addresses only Eliphaz. Why? Eliphaz was the eldest, the leader, the one who spoke first, the most prolific. He was also, interestingly, the kindest of the three — less harsh than Bildad and Zophar. And yet God addresses him by name. God says: you're wrong, my wrath is aroused against you.

Second, note who is *not* named: Elihu. He is conspicuously absent from the review. We noted earlier that this suggests Elihu was largely right — that his contribution was not the same as the three friends', and that God does not rebuke him.

**On Job's vindication:** God says the friends "have not spoken of Me what is right, as My servant Job has." You might ask: but wasn't Job shaking his fist at God? Wasn't he trying to bring a lawsuit against God? How can God say Job was right?

Here is the key: Job was not right in everything he said. But on the central issue — the issue he was fighting with the friends about — he was right. What were the friends saying? *You are suffering because you have sinned. Only the wicked suffer. The righteous are always blessed.* Job said: *No — the righteous can also suffer. Suffering is not only the result of personal sin.* And on that issue, God says: Job is right, and you are wrong.

Job was also right in saying these things to the friends' faces. He was not gossiping. He was not complaining behind God's back. He was directing his lament to God, acknowledging God's sovereignty even in his anger. That was honest faith — misguided, but honest.

## Part 2: Job's Intercession for His Friends

Now comes one of the most beautiful moments in the book:

> *"My servant Job shall pray for you."* (Job 42:8)

Think about this. Job's three friends have been tormenting him for the entire book — accusing him of secret sin, telling him he deserves worse suffering than he's received, trying to convince him that God is punishing him. And now God says: Job, you are going to intercede for them. You are going to pray for the people who wronged you.

Does that call anyone else to mind?

It is almost impossible not to be reminded of the other Servant of the Lord — who made atonement for His people and intercedes for them even as they sin against Him. *"Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they do."*

Notice the irony as well: Job spent much of the book crying out for a *mediator* — someone who could stand between him and God, lay a hand on them both, and bring reconciliation. And now God says: *you* are going to be the mediator. Job, who longed for a go-between, is now appointed to stand between God and his friends, offering sacrifice on their behalf and interceding for them.

> *"For there is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus, who gave Himself a ransom for all."* (1 Timothy 2:5–6)

Job is a type of Christ here: wronged, yet called to intercede; sinned against, yet appointed to pray for those who wronged him.

The friends show their repentance in their actions — verse 9 says they *went and did as the Lord commanded them.* They obeyed immediately, demonstrating genuine repentance, and God accepted them.

This section of the epilogue displays a very basic element of the gospel: there is hope of restoration for all sinners. There is never a moment of hopelessness, as long as someone is breathing. The Lord is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in mercy. He has not dealt with us according to our sins, nor punished us according to our iniquities. Our God is gracious, and there is always hope for a sinner because of God's disposition toward those who come to Him.

## Part 3: Job's Restoration (Job 42:10–17)

> *"And the Lord restored Job's losses when he prayed for his friends. Indeed the Lord gave Job twice as much as he had before."* (Job 42:10)

Some commentators are disappointed with this ending. They argue it would be more consistent with the book's message if Job had simply died in misery — that restoring his wealth perpetuates the very retribution theology the book was meant to refute. But I don't think that is what is happening here.

Job's restoration is not provided to answer all questions about his specific suffering experience. Rather, it reveals a universal principle — a principle of righteous suffering in the lives of all believers. By giving Job a *double portion*, God was showing that He plans a disproportionate response of blessing for all who suffer righteously.

> *"For our light and momentary troubles are achieving for us an eternal glory that far outweighs them all. So we fix our eyes not on what is seen, but on what is unseen, since what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal."* (2 Corinthians 4:17–18)

That double portion — that disproportionate weight of glory — is the life to come.

Note also: Job had seven sons and three daughters again, exactly the same number as before. But this is not a replacement. No amount of new children could make Job forget the ten he lost. He will live the rest of his days knowing the pain of what happened, even if he does not know why. But now he is willing to trust that the Lord knew what He was doing.

## Three Final Lessons from the Book of Job

**First: The righteous do suffer in this life.** This book clears up the false notion that suffering is always tied to personal sin. Because of this, it helps us to be more compassionate when we deal with suffering people — they may not simply be reaping what they sowed. And it brings meaning to the suffering of believers: God has a purpose in our suffering, and He is sovereign over it.

**Second: Job teaches us how to be spiritually prepared for suffering.** Job may not be the best book to read in the midst of suffering — but it does help us prepare preventatively. Look at how Job is described at the beginning of chapter 1: he is already praying, already offering sacrifice, already interceding for his children. He is using the ordinary means of grace. The best way to prepare for suffering is to walk ordinarily with the Lord in the ways He has established for us: the reading and especially the preaching of the Word on the Lord's Day, prayer, the Lord's Supper, baptism, fellowship with the body of Christ. These ordinary means are how God prepares His church for everything He calls it to do.

**Third: Job teaches us to look to Jesus.** Jesus is the ultimate answer for suffering. How does God deal with suffering, with evil, with wickedness? On the cross. That is how He deals with it. The cross establishes the clear principle that a righteous man may suffer terribly according to the good and redemptive purpose of a loving and just God.

Job's suffering was redemptive — there was purpose, there was fruit. The same with the Lord Jesus Christ. He did not deserve to suffer. He was righteous. But He suffered for us — the righteous for the unrighteous — and because Christ suffered well, we too can suffer well. He equips us by His Spirit to suffer as He did. That is the point of 1 Peter 2 — we are called to suffer well, because our Lord suffered for us and He goes with us.

This is the majestic book of Job. All of Scripture is inspired by God and profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, thoroughly equipped for every good work — and that includes the book of Job.
